summary: Using the Away CEO controversy and his own career arc, Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s obsession with growth turns ambition into a default, socially enforced scoreboard. He contends that our ambitions are largely circumstantial—shaped by the environments we immerse ourselves in—and warns that we can spend years climbing ladders we didn’t consciously choose.
money_quotes: In Silicon Valley, we’ve drowned ourselves so deeply in this ethos that we no longer realize that we’re in the water.
We created them to manufacture accomplishment for ourselves.

Standing on the shore, your old pond—the pond where you did your life's work, in which nothing seemed more important than being a big fish—suddenly looks like all the other ponds you used to care so little about.
But at a minimum, we shouldn’t find our ambitions by accident.
key_insight: Ambition in tech is often an environmental artifact, so without deliberate choice we end up chasing success metrics that the ecosystem hands us rather than the ones we truly want.
